hub_subtitle: From hesitant communicator to confident global business leader

lesson.menu_title: Financial Discussions — Budgets, Forecasts & the Third Conditional
lesson.menu_desc: Talk money with confidence on a quarterly review call with Karen: present a budget, read a forecast, and explain what drives the profit on a collection — expenses, overhead, margins and cash flow — then look back at a costly mistake and discuss what you would have done differently using the third conditional (if + past perfect, would have + participle), the natural language of reviewing financial decisions — 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 17 — Financial Discussions — Budgets, Forecasts & the Third Conditional
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Milton Sayegh | Aula 17 — Financial Discussions | Business English for Luxury Export | Alumni by Better

listenings.text: Hi Milton, it is Karen, just leaving you a quick voice note before our quarterly review tomorrow so the numbers are not a surprise. The good news first: revenue is up, and the spring collection came in under budget, which gives us a healthy surplus to reinvest. The forecast for next quarter looks strong too, around fifteen percent growth if the new pieces sell the way we expect. The one thing I want to flag is overhead. The shipping and the customs expenses crept up again, and they are eating into the profit on every order. I think if we allocate a little more to logistics up front, we will protect the margin later. Take a look at the projection I sent, especially the cash flow on page two, and come ready to talk about where we trim and where we invest. No pressure, I just know how far your English has come, and honestly I am looking forward to a real strategy conversation. Talk tomorrow.

Hey, this is Milton, a quick voice note to myself after the quarterly review. It went well, but it made me think about last year. If I had read the forecast more carefully back then, I would have seen the overhead problem coming months earlier. If we had allocated more to shipping from the start, we would not have lost so much margin on the winter orders, and the profit would have looked very different. Honestly, if I had spoken better English a year ago, I would have pushed back on that supplier contract instead of just nodding along. The good thing is, today I can actually have this conversation. I read the projection, I questioned the expenses, and I explained the cash flow to Karen without freezing once. The takeaway is simple. The numbers were never really the hard part. The hard part was finding the words, and now I have them. Next quarter, if a forecast looks off, I will say so on the call, in the moment, in English.
